label: SES-135 / CNV-037 close-out — YAML schema v1.3 Section 12 (RBAC) drafting. RE-KEYED from a parallel SES-133/CNV-035 on origin/main that collided with the PI-112 redesign's identifiers: SES-133->SES-135, CNV-035->CNV-037, DEC-345..347->DEC-350..352, WS-015->PRJ-017 (Project), and references reshaped to the post-rename model (conversation_belongs_to_project, session_belongs_to_project). Records 3 decisions (Section 12 placement, no file-type discriminator, content-based deploy ordering). Belongs to Project PRJ-017. The schema content (06c3605) is already an ancestor on main.

session.session_title: YAML schema v1.3 Section 12 drafted — Role-Based Access Control Parts A–E added to app-yaml-schema.md
session.session_executive_summary: A Claude.ai conversation executing the v1.2 Category 6 drafting kickoff from SES-053. It translated the v1.2-scope half of the MR-pilot gap analysis Section 6 into a new Section 12 of app-yaml-schema.md (Role-Based Access Control), bumped the document v1.2.5 -> v1.3, and resolved three architecture questions: Section 12 placement as a new top-level section; roles:/teams: as optional top-level keys rather than a new file-type; and content-based loader discovery for deploy ordering. One commit landed (06c3605) adding Section 12 (Roles, Teams, Entity Access, System Permissions, Role-Aware Visibility, Deploy Ordering) plus extensions to Sections 3.1/7.1/10/11. Re-keyed from a parallel SES-133 that collided with the PI-112 redesign identifiers.
session.session_description: RE-KEYED close-out of the YAML schema v1.3 Section 12 (RBAC) drafting session, originally authored as SES-133/CNV-035 on origin/main in parallel with the PI-112 governance redesign, which claimed the same identifiers (SES-133, CNV-035, DEC-345..347, WS-015). Re-keyed to next-free identifiers and reshaped to the renamed Project model to resolve the collision. Single Claude.ai planning conversation. CNV-035. New parent Project WS-015 (YAML Schema v1.3 — Role-Based Access Control). Medium: chat (claude.ai). Seed prompt: "Please process all prompts without asking me any issues, and I confirm" — Doug's confirmation on the operative crmbuilder/CLAUDE.md and waiver of the kickoff's stop-and-ask gate for the three pre-drafting decisions. Pre-flight execution followed the kickoff's order: read crmbuilder/CLAUDE.md, then yaml-schema-gap-analysis-MR-pilot.md Section 6 (lines 618–1034 covering the full Parts A–E plus deferred-to-v1.3 section plus Q1–Q8 resolutions), then app-yaml-schema.md head and the touch-point sections (3.1 top-level properties, 5 entity block, 7 layouts, 10 validation including 10.1 NOT_SUPPORTED handling, 11 condition expressions), then espo_impl/core/config_loader.py to confirm the current top-level key set (version, content_version, description, entities, relationships). The three pre-drafting decisions were resolved and announced briefly per Doug's process-without-asking instruction and the ARCHITECTURE-mode decide-and-announce path of the two-part test. Section placement (DEC-345) — new top-level Section 12 mirroring Parts A–E. File-type concept (DEC-346) — no discriminator; roles: and teams: become optional top-level keys alongside entities: and relationships:. Deploy ordering (DEC-347) — loader-level content-based discovery; any file declaring roles: or teams: queued ahead of files referencing them. One structural reconciliation surfaced and was resolved inline: the gap-analysis Part E.2 sketch shows `layouts:` (plural list) at the entity level, but the current schema uses `layout:` (singular map keyed by layout type — layout.detail, layout.list). Reconciled by extending the value at each layout.{type} key from 'a single layout block' (v1.0–v1.2.5 form) to 'either a single layout block OR a list of role-scoped variants, each with required forRoles:.' The two forms are mutually exclusive at any given layout.{type} key; the v1.0–v1.2.5 form is preserved as the default. Q5 coverage rule (every role appears in exactly one variant; no role doubly matched) applied at the layout-{type} key level. Doc revision: v1.2.5 → v1.3 (the gap analysis's 'v1.2' and 'v1.3' labels are feature-scope, not doc-revision; the v1.2.x patches accumulated past v1.2 and Parts A–E is the next minor bump). Drafting was executed in six passes against app-yaml-schema.md: header version + Last Updated bump; revision-history table row; Section 3.1 roles/teams rows + revised file-shape principle; Section 7.1 layout-variant alternative pointer; Section 10 new validation block (Role-level, Team-level, Layout-forRoles-level, Top-level v1.3+) inserted before Section 10.1; Section 11 leaf-clause split (intro update, Section 11.2 rewrite for field vs role clauses, Section 11.5 rejection rules for role: clauses in non-visibleWhen contexts); appended full Section 12 (12.1–12.7). Final document state: 2786 lines (was 2138 — net +648 lines), single commit 06c3605 pushed direct to origin. No application code touched; no CBM repo edits; no Claude Code prompt series produced — the implementation prompt series is the next-conversation artifact. Post-push: in the sandbox attempt to push the close-out artifacts the working tree discovered origin had advanced from SES-053 to SES-132 (the governance entity schema workstream had executed and the schema migrated to the session_identifier/conversation/work_ticket/commits shape; areas restructured; workstream→Project rename in flight). The schema-spec commit 06c3605 survived as an ancestor of origin/main HEAD; the original SES-054-shaped close-out payload was abandoned and this SES-133 payload authored against the current shape with re-projected identifiers.

conversation.conversation_title: Drafting YAML schema v1.3 Section 12 — Role-Based Access Control
conversation.conversation_purpose: Execute the v1.2 Category 6 drafting kickoff (PRDs/product/UPDATE-PROMPT-yaml-schema-v1.2-category-6.md from SES-053): translate yaml-schema-gap-analysis-MR-pilot.md Section 6 into a new Section 12 of app-yaml-schema.md, resolve the three pre-drafting decisions the kickoff flagged, and ship a single schema-spec commit bumping the doc from v1.2.5 to v1.3.
conversation.conversation_description: Started by reading the operative CLAUDE.md, the gap-analysis Section 6 authoritative source, the existing app-yaml-schema.md touch-point sections, and the loader to confirm the top-level key set. Resolved three pre-drafting decisions inline: Section 12 as a new top-level section mirroring Parts A–E; no file-type discriminator with roles:/teams: added as optional top-level keys; content-based deploy ordering. Reconciled one structural conflict between the gap-analysis Part E.2 sketch (which used layouts: plural-list) and the current schema (layout: singular-map) by allowing the value at each layout.{type} key to be either a single block or a list of role-scoped variants, with Q5 coverage applied per-layout-type. Edited app-yaml-schema.md in six passes (header bump, revision-history row, Section 3.1 extension, Section 7.1 variant alternative, Section 10 new validation block, Section 11 leaf-clause split, full Section 12 appended). Net diff +657 / -8 lines, single commit 06c3605 pushed direct to origin. No application code touched. Post-push discovered origin had advanced past the pre-flight snapshot; rebuilt this close-out against the current governance schema with re-projected identifiers.

commits.commit_message_first_line: yaml-schema v1.3: add Section 12 — Role-Based Access Control (Category 6 Parts A–E)
commits.commit_message_full:
yaml-schema v1.3: add Section 12 — Role-Based Access Control (Category 6 Parts A–E)

Adds the v1.2-scope half of Category 6 per yaml-schema-gap-analysis-MR-pilot.md
Section 6, as amended 05-20-26 21:18 (Option C reordering).

New top-level keys: roles: and teams: (Section 3.1 updated). New Section 12
ties Parts A–E together:
- 12.1 Roles (name as identity, persona as documentation metadata)
- 12.2 Teams (membership as runtime data, out of YAML scope)
- 12.3 Scope-Level Entity Access (whitelist semantics per Q8)
- 12.4 System Permissions (v1.3 enumeration with per-key value vocabulary)
- 12.5 Role-Aware Visibility (12.5.1 role: leaf clause in Section 11;
  12.5.2 forRoles: on layout-type variants with Q5 coverage rule)
- 12.6 Deploy Ordering (content-based queueing of roles/teams files)
- 12.7 Deferred (field-level permissions and presets — future schema bump)

Section 7.1 extended: layout.{type} value may now be either a single
layout block (legacy) or a list of role-scoped variants. Section 11.2
extended with role: leaf clause; Section 11.5 adds rejection rules for
role: clauses in non-visibleWhen contexts. Section 10 adds Role/Team/
forRoles validation block.

Field-level permissions and named presets (gap-analysis Section 6
'Deferred to v1.3') remain out of scope here. The gap analysis's 'v1.2'
and 'v1.3' labels are feature-scope ordering, not doc revision numbers;
Parts A–E land as the next minor doc bump (v1.2.5 → v1.3).

No application code changed. Implementation prompt series is a future
artifact, not in this session's scope.

decisions.title: Section 12 placement: new top-level section, not slotted under existing structure
decisions.context: First of three pre-drafting decisions the SES-053 kickoff flagged. Category 6 Parts A–E touch multiple existing sections of app-yaml-schema.md — Part A and B add top-level keys (roles:, teams:); Part C adds per-role entity-name-keyed blocks; Part D adds per-role flag and scope-style permissions; Part E.1 extends Section 11's condition-expression leaf-clause grammar; Part E.2 extends Section 7's layout shape. The question: single new top-level Section 12, or slot each Part into the section it most closely touches.
decisions.decision: Adopt a single new top-level Section 12 'Security and Role-Based Access Control' with seven subsections (12.1 Roles, 12.2 Teams, 12.3 Scope-Level Entity Access, 12.4 System Permissions, 12.5 Role-Aware Visibility with 12.5.1 role: leaf clauses and 12.5.2 layout-level role scoping, 12.6 Deploy Ordering, 12.7 Deferred — Field-Level Permissions and Permission Presets). The touched existing sections (3.1, 7.1, 10, 11) carry brief cross-references forward to Section 12; the authoritative spec for every Part lives in Section 12.
decisions.alternatives_considered: Slotted under existing structure — Part A and B extend Section 3.1's top-level properties table with full prose; Part C inserts a new subsection inside Section 5 entity-block prose; Part D inserts a sibling subsection there; Part E.1 extends Section 11.2 and 11.5 with the full role: clause specification inline; Part E.2 inserts a new Section 7.7 with the forRoles: specification. Defensible because it keeps the additions adjacent to the constructs they extend. Rejected because (a) Section 5 is the entity-block specification and the entity-block-internal additions are smaller than the cross-cutting role and team declarations that have no entity-block home; (b) the conceptual cohesion of RBAC is lost; (c) a future reader trying to understand the security model would have to navigate five disjoint sections; (d) the existing Section 11 precedent argues for cross-cutting top-level sections.
decisions.consequences: Section 12 appended to app-yaml-schema.md at lines 2263–2786 (commit 06c3605). Sections 3.1, 7.1, 10, and 11 carry forward-links to Section 12 subsections. Future RBAC schema additions land in Section 12; if the deferred field-level + presets work lands per gap-analysis Section 6, it extends Section 12 (likely as a new 12.8 or by rewriting the 12.7 forward reference into an active specification). The v1.3 implementation prompt series (next conversation) reads Section 12 as the authoritative spec for what the loader, validator, and deploy managers must enforce.
decisions.executive_summary: Where the new Role-Based Access Control material lives in the schema spec. New self-contained Section 12 (with sub-sections per Part) rather than splitting across existing sections. Keeps RBAC together as one conceptual unit and follows the precedent set by Section 11 (Shared Condition Expressions, the other cross-cutting top-level section).

decisions.title: No file-type discriminator: roles: and teams: are optional top-level keys, not a new program-file type
decisions.context: Second of three pre-drafting decisions the SES-053 kickoff flagged. The gap-analysis Section 6 describes a 'dedicated security file' at programs/security.yaml. Two reconciliations are possible: introduce a new program-file type (a 'security file' with required roles: and/or teams: and no entities:/relationships:, identified by a file-type discriminator), or extend the existing single program-file type to permit roles: and teams: as optional top-level keys alongside entities: and relationships:.
decisions.decision: No file-type discriminator. roles: and teams: are added as optional top-level keys alongside the existing entities: and relationships:. The existing single program-file type extends to permit any combination of the four data keys; a file with all four, with only roles:/teams:, with only entities:, or with no data keys at all is structurally valid. programs/security.yaml is a recommended operational convention (documented in Section 12 and Section 3.1 prose), not enforced by the schema. The loader does not key behaviour off the filename.
decisions.alternatives_considered: File-type discriminator — introduce file_type: security as a required top-level key on the dedicated security file, with file_type: program (implicit default) for entity/relationship files; the validator would hard-reject a file that mixes roles:/teams: with entities:/relationships: under either file_type. Rejected because (a) the existing schema doesn't discriminate file types and adding one now is a backward-incompatible expansion; (b) the mixing case is rare and the cost of policing it is high; (c) the operator-error case is already mitigated by the convention of programs/security.yaml being a distinct file; (d) the loader and validator surface stays smaller without the discriminator. Filename-pattern enforcement — the loader recognizes files matching programs/security.yaml as security files and rejects entities:/relationships: blocks in them; non-security files are rejected if they declare roles:/teams:. Rejected because it makes filename a semantic key, brittle under rename and surprising to operators.
decisions.consequences: Section 3.1 top-level-properties table extended with roles: and teams: rows. Post-table prose rewritten to permit any combination of the four data keys, with the convention of programs/security.yaml documented but not enforced. Section 10 top-level validation block (v1.3+) extends the existing 'at least one' rule to cover the four data keys. config_loader.py extension (next conversation's implementation work) adds two pass-through parse paths for roles: and teams: top-level keys; no file-type registry surface introduced. The single-file mixing case is documented as 'allowed but recommended-against' in Section 12.6 prose.
decisions.executive_summary: Whether the schema invents a new 'security file' type for the new roles: and teams: keys. No — they slot in as two more optional top-level keys alongside entities: and relationships:. Existing single-file type just grows by two keys. programs/security.yaml is a convention, not a schema-level distinction. Matches the principle the schema already uses ('a file may contain entities, relationships, or both') and avoids inventing a file-type registry for no real benefit.

decisions.title: Deploy ordering: content-based loader discovery, not filename-based or pipeline convention
decisions.context: Third of three pre-drafting decisions the SES-053 kickoff flagged. Gap-analysis Section 6 states that programs/security.yaml must be applied before any domain program file (roles and teams must exist on the target CRM before any field, layout, or scope grant references them). Three mechanisms could enforce that ordering: (a) schema-level constraint via a file-type discriminator that signals the file must run first (rejected by DEC-346's no-discriminator decision); (b) deployment-pipeline-level convention where the operator orders the batch correctly and the pipeline trusts the order; or (c) loader-level discovery rule where any file declaring roles: or teams: is automatically queued ahead of files referencing them, regardless of filename or position in the batch.
decisions.decision: Loader-level discovery rule, content-based. Any file in the deploy batch that declares either roles: or teams: top-level keys is queued ahead of files that do not. The loader scans the batch at pre-flight time and computes the order from content. Filename is not consulted. Within the leading group, teams are deployed before roles by operational convention; the YAML has no cross-reference between roles: and teams: at the schema level (a scope_access: read: team value is a scope vocabulary, not a team-name binding), so the within-group order is conventional rather than required. Documented in new Section 12.6.
decisions.alternatives_considered: Filename-based — loader treats files matching programs/security.yaml as ordering-priority-1; everything else is priority-2. Rejected because filename is not a semantic key in the existing schema and elevating it now would diverge from the rest of the design. Pipeline convention — the Configure UI documents the rule ('list security.yaml first in your batch') but the pipeline trusts the order. Rejected because the failure mode (missing role at deploy time) is downstream of the misordering and hard to diagnose; the schema's hard-reject discipline argues for catching the dependency at pre-flight rather than relying on operator discipline. Schema-level via discriminator — already rejected by DEC-346.
decisions.consequences: Documented in new Section 12.6 'Deploy Ordering'. The v1.3 implementation prompt series (next conversation) extends the existing batch-loading code in espo_impl/core/config_loader.py / the Configure pipeline to scan each file's top-level keys before computing the run order, queueing any file with roles: or teams: declarations ahead of files that declare only entities:/relationships:. Cross-file role-name resolution is added to ProgramContext following the existing field-name resolution model (commit d98db71, 05-04-26). The pre-flight validator emits a hard-reject for any file in the batch that references a role name no file declares.
decisions.executive_summary: How the deploy pipeline knows to apply role and team declarations before the files that reference them. The loader scans each file's top-level keys at pre-flight and queues any file declaring roles: or teams: ahead of files that don't — regardless of filename or batch position. Filename-based and operator-managed ordering both rejected because they would let mis-ordering through to deploy-time failures.
